title: When Batch Size Is Small
concept: BatchNorm can struggle when mini-batches are tiny because its batch statistics get noisy, which is why alternatives like GroupNorm and LayerNorm are often preferred in small-batch CNN workloads.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does BatchNorm use running averages at inference time?

options.text: So predictions do not depend on random neighboring examples
So the model can skip hidden layers forever
So training data can be shuffled into a new class order

exercises.explain: Correct — inference should stay stable even for one example at a time.

cards.title: Polling only two people
cards.body: If you ask only two people what the whole city thinks, your estimate may bounce around wildly depending on who those two happen to be. BatchNorm has a similar problem when the mini-batch is tiny: its 'what is normal right now?' guess gets noisy.

cards.title: Tiny batches give shaky estimates
cards.body: BatchNorm works best when the current mini-batch gives a decent snapshot of the activations. With batch size 1 or 2, the batch's average and spread can jump around a lot, so the normalization itself becomes noisy. That can make training less stable instead of more stable.

cards.title: Judging a whole season from one game
cards.body: Imagine deciding a basketball player's typical skill from just one or two possessions. You might overreact to a lucky shot or a bad bounce. Small-batch BatchNorm can overreact in the same way because the reference is too tiny.

cards.title: Huge images force tiny batches
cards.body: In medical imaging, detection, or satellite vision, one image can be so large that a GPU only fits one or two at a time. In that setting, BatchNorm may wobble because the batch statistics are weak. GroupNorm and LayerNorm are popular alternatives because they normalize within each example or within channel groups instead of leaning so hard on the batch dimension.

cards.title: BatchNorm is not bad everywhere
cards.body: This is not a claim that BatchNorm is obsolete. With moderate or large mini-batches, it is still widely useful. The real lesson is simpler: the right normalization depends on the workload, and tiny-batch CNNs often need a different tool.

left.text:
Current batch may be unrepresentative.
Normalization can wobble from step to step.
Training may feel noisy.

right.text:
Depend less on the batch dimension.
More predictable with batch size 1 or 2.
Often a better fit for memory-heavy CNNs.

exercises.prompt: Why can BatchNorm struggle when batch size is only 1 or 2?

options.text: Because the batch statistics can be too noisy or unrepresentative to give a stable normalization reference.
Because convolutions only work when batch size is at least 16.
Because feature maps disappear when memory is low.
Because GroupNorm deletes all channel information.

exercises.explain: Exactly — with tiny batches, the reference BatchNorm depends on can get shaky.

exercises.prompt: True or false: GroupNorm and LayerNorm are useful partly because they rely less on batch-wide statistics than BatchNorm does.
exercises.explain: True — that is a big reason they show up in small-batch workloads.

exercises.sentence: When batches are tiny, BatchNorm's statistics can become ___.
exercises.explain: Noisy is right — the reference itself can wobble when the batch is too small.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best explains why a team might switch away from BatchNorm?

options.text: A segmentation model can only fit one huge image per GPU step, so the team chooses GroupNorm for a steadier normalization signal.
A CNN uses three-by-three kernels, so normalization is mathematically forbidden.
A model stores its weights on disk between training runs.

exercises.explain: Yes — tiny-batch vision work is exactly where GroupNorm or LayerNorm often make more sense.

exercises.prompt: A detection model trains on giant images and can only fit two images per mini-batch. Which normalization choice best fits that constraint?

options.text: Consider GroupNorm or LayerNorm, because BatchNorm's tiny-batch statistics may be too noisy to be reliable.
Stick with BatchNorm because tiny batches always improve its estimates.
Remove normalization entirely because CNNs never benefit from it.
Replace every convolution with pooling so batch size no longer matters.

exercises.explain: Exactly. BatchNorm is great in many settings, but tiny batches can make its reference unstable. Next, we'll zoom out and ask a bigger design question: what normalization really gives you — and what it does not.
